metrics.reason: Shows whether the Redis systemd unit is active (running) or not – the primary indicator of the failure.
Indicates if the unit file was successfully loaded; a failed load could prevent the service from starting.
Shows how long the service has been up; a value of 0 confirms it never started or stopped recently.
Verifies the unit file state (enabled/disabled/static); mis‑configuration can stop the service from being started automatically.
CPU usage of the Redis process – helps detect if the process is alive but stuck or throttled.
Resident memory size of the Redis process; abnormal values may indicate OOM or memory‑related crashes.
Virtual memory size of the Redis process; useful for spotting memory leaks before the process dies.
Number of Redis processes; zero confirms the process is not running.
Uptime reported by Redis itself; if the service is down this metric will be missing or stale.
Version information – useful when correlating known bugs with the observed failure.
Total memory used by Redis; high usage can trigger OOM kills causing the service to stop.
Resident set size of Redis memory; helps differentiate between allocated vs. actually used memory.
Configured maxmemory limit; if reached Redis may start evicting keys or abort.
Memory fragmentation; extreme fragmentation can cause performance degradation and crashes.
Number of client connections; a sudden drop to zero may indicate the server stopped accepting connections.
Number of blocked clients; high values can indicate long‑running commands that may stall the server.
Cumulative command count; a flat line suggests the server is not processing any commands.
Current operations per second; useful to see if the service was under heavy load before stopping.
Cache hit count; helps assess workload characteristics before the outage.
Cache miss count; together with hits can indicate abnormal access patterns preceding failure.
Timestamp of the last successful RDB snapshot; if too old, persistence may be failing and causing restarts.
Result of the last background save (OK/ERR); errors may have caused the service to abort.
Indicates whether a background save is currently running; a stuck save can block the process.
Shows if the instance is master or replica; role changes can trigger restarts.
Number of connected replicas; a sudden drop may indicate network or replication issues causing the master to stop.
Overall CPU utilization; high CPU can cause the Redis process to be killed or fail to start.
1‑minute load average; spikes may correlate with the service crash.
Overall memory usage; low free memory can lead to OOM termination of Redis.
Amount of memory available for new processes; helps verify if memory pressure caused the stop.
Free swap space; insufficient swap can exacerbate memory pressure.
I/O utilization of the primary disk; high I/O can delay Redis writes and trigger failures.
I/O utilization of the secondary disk (if used for persistence); same rationale as above.
Incoming network traffic; a drop may indicate network issues preventing Redis from accepting connections.
Outgoing network traffic; helps correlate network saturation with service failure.
